This is a smart approach to free up space on your C drive. Yes, moving your VS Code profile (extensions, settings, etc.) to the S drive via a symbolic link is a common practice.

However, there are a few issues with the command you provided:

1. **The `<you>` placeholder** - You need to replace this with your actual Windows username
2. **You must first move the existing data** before creating the symlink

Here's the correct process:

**Step 1: Find your Windows username**
Run this in cmd.exe:
```cmd
echo %USERNAME%
```

**Step 2: The complete process**
```cmd
REM 1. First, close VS Code completely

REM 2. Copy the existing Code folder to S drive (this may take a while)
xcopy /E /I /H /Y "C:\Users\YOUR_USERNAME\AppData\Roaming\Code" "S:\VSCodeProfile"

REM 3. Delete the original folder (after confirming copy succeeded)
rmdir /S /Q "C:\Users\YOUR_USERNAME\AppData\Roaming\Code"

REM 4. Create the symbolic link (run as Administrator)
mklink /D "C:\Users\YOUR_USERNAME\AppData\Roaming\Code" "S:\VSCodeProfile"
```

Would you like me to:
1. **Check your Windows username** and help construct the exact commands?
2. **Look at your existing `S_drive_map.txt`** to see what you've already moved there?

The symbolic link approach is good because:
- VS Code will transparently use the S drive location
- Your C drive stays clean
- It's reversible if needed

Just let me know your Windows username and I'll provide the exact commands to run.

Your username is **seand**. Yes, moving VS Code's profile data to your S drive is a good idea. Here's the exact process:

**Important: Close VS Code completely before doing this!**

Run these commands in Command Prompt (as Administrator):

```cmd
REM 1. Copy existing Code folder to S drive
xcopy /E /I /H /Y "C:\Users\seand\AppData\Roaming\Code" "S:\VSCodeProfile"

REM 2. Delete the original folder (after confirming copy succeeded)
rmdir /S /Q "C:\Users\seand\AppData\Roaming\Code"

REM 3. Create the symbolic link
mklink /D "C:\Users\seand\AppData\Roaming\Code" "S:\VSCodeProfile"
```

Or as a single PowerShell script (run as Administrator):
```powershell
# Close VS Code first!
Copy-Item -Path "C:\Users\seand\AppData\Roaming\Code" -Destination "S:\VSCodeProfile" -Recurse -Force
Remove-Item -Path "C:\Users\seand\AppData\Roaming\Code" -Recurse -Force
New-Item -ItemType SymbolicLink -Path "C:\Users\seand\AppData\Roaming\Code" -Target "S:\VSCodeProfile"
```

The symbolic link makes VS Code transparently use the S drive location, keeping your C drive clean. This is reversible if needed.

input.result:
Your username is **seand**. Yes, moving VS Code's profile to your S drive is a smart move to free up C drive space.

**Run these in Command Prompt (as Administrator):**

1. First, close VS Code completely

2. Copy the Code folder to S drive:
```cmd
xcopy /E /I /H /Y "C:\Users\seand\AppData\Roaming\Code" "S:\VSCodeProfile"
```

3. Delete the original folder (after confirming copy worked):
```cmd
rmdir /S /Q "C:\Users\seand\AppData\Roaming\Code"
```

4. Create the symbolic link:
```cmd
mklink /D "C:\Users\seand\AppData\Roaming\Code" "S:\VSCodeProfile"
```

VS Code will transparently use the S drive location after this. The symbolic link approach is fully reversible if needed.

**Active Project Folders (root level):**
```
s:/

├── agent2/            # Parallel Kilo instance 2  
├── agent3/            # Parallel Kilo instance 3
├── backend/           # API routes
├── clients/           # LLM client implementations
├── cockpit/          # MAIN - Cockpit UI
├── config/            # All config files
├── data/              # Database files
├── logs/              # Log files
├── models/            # AI models
├── orchestrator/      # Core orchestration
├── redis/             # Memurai/Redis
├── scripts/           # Startup scripts
└── services/         # Shared services
```

**Backed Up (safe to delete later if everything works):**
- `backup_frontend_old/` - Old frontend (replaced by cockpit/ui)
- `backup_static_old/` - Old static files
- `backup_tools_old/` - Old tools
- `backup_samples/` - Old samples
- `backup_vector/` - Old vector stuff
- `backup_temp/` - Old temp files

File: scripts/boot-cockpit.ps1
Lines 1-114:
  1 | # scripts/boot-cockpit.ps1
  2 | # FreeAgent Swarm - Single Command Boot
  3 | param(
  4 |     [switch]$NoBrowser,
  5 |     [switch]$SkipKill = $false
  6 | )
  7 | 
  8 | Write-Host ""
  9 | Write-Host "========================================" -ForegroundColor Cyan
 10 | Write-Host "  FREEAGENT SWARM BOOT SEQUENCE   " -ForegroundColor Cyan
 11 | Write-Host "========================================" -ForegroundColor Cyan
 12 | Write-Host ""
 13 | 
 14 | # Always run from repo root
 15 | $rootDir = Split-Path $PSScriptRoot -Parent
 16 | if (-not $rootDir) { $rootDir = $PSScriptRoot }
 17 | Set-Location $rootDir
 18 | $scriptDir = Join-Path $rootDir "scripts"
 19 | 
 20 | # Colors
 21 | $Cyan = "Cyan"
 22 | $Yellow = "Yellow"
 23 | $Green = "Green"
 24 | $Red = "Red"
 25 | $Gray = "Gray"
 26 | 
 27 | function Write-Step {
 28 |     param([string]$Message, [string]$Color = "White")
 29 |     Write-Host "[$(Get-Date -Format 'HH:mm:ss')] $Message" -ForegroundColor $Color
 30 | }
 31 | 
 32 | function Start-NodeProcess {
 33 |     param(
 34 |         [string]$Name,
 35 |         [string]$ScriptPath,
 36 |         [string]$Args = ""
 37 |     )
 38 |     $fullPath = Join-Path $rootDir $ScriptPath
 39 |     if (Test-Path $fullPath) {
 40 |         Write-Host "  [OK] $Name" -ForegroundColor $Green
 41 |         Start-Process powershell -WindowStyle Normal -ArgumentList "-NoExit", "-Command", "cd '$rootDir'; node `"$ScriptPath`" $Args" 
 42 |     } else {
 43 |         Write-Host "  [WARN] $Name - $ScriptPath not found" -ForegroundColor Yellow
 44 |     }
 45 | }
 46 | 
 47 | # ============================================
 48 | # STEP 1: Kill Old Processes
 49 | # ============================================
 50 | Write-Step "[1/7] Killing old processes / ports..." $Yellow
 51 | if (-not $SkipKill) {
 52 |     & "$scriptDir\kill-ports.ps1"
 53 | } else {
 54 |     Write-Step "  Skipped (using -SkipKill)" $Gray
 55 | }
 56 | 
 57 | # ============================================
 58 | # STEP 2: Start Redis / Memurai
 59 | # ============================================
 60 | Write-Step "[2/7] Starting Redis / Memurai..." $Yellow
 61 | & "$scriptDir\start-redis.ps1"
 62 | Start-Sleep -Seconds 2
 63 | 
 64 | # ============================================
 65 | # STEP 3: Start Core Services
 66 | # ============================================
 67 | Write-Step "[3/7] Starting core services..." $Yellow
 68 | & "$scriptDir\start-services.ps1"
 69 | 
 70 | # ============================================
 71 | # STEP 4: Start Orchestrator
 72 | # ============================================
 73 | Write-Step "[4/7] Starting orchestrator..." $Yellow
 74 | Start-NodeProcess -Name "Orchestrator" -ScriptPath "orchestrator/orchestrator.js"
 75 | 
 76 | # ============================================
 77 | # STEP 5: Start Router Agent
 78 | # ============================================
 79 | Write-Step "[5/7] Starting router agent..." $Yellow
 80 | Start-NodeProcess -Name "RouterAgent" -ScriptPath "services/routerAgent.js"
 81 | 
 82 | # ============================================
 83 | # STEP 6: Start Provider Adapters
 84 | # ============================================
 85 | Write-Step "[6/7] Starting provider adapters..." $Yellow
 86 | Start-NodeProcess -Name "ClawAdapter" -ScriptPath "services/clawAdapter.js"
 87 | Start-NodeProcess -Name "KiloAgent" -ScriptPath "services/kiloAgent.js"
 88 | 
 89 | # ============================================
 90 | # STEP 7: Start Main Server
 91 | # ============================================
 92 | Write-Step "[7/7] Starting main server..." $Yellow
 93 | Start-NodeProcess -Name "MainServer" -ScriptPath "server.js"
 94 |

 97 | Write-Host "  Swarm online!" -ForegroundColor Green
 98 | Write-Host "========================================" -ForegroundColor Green
 99 | Write-Host ""
100 | Write-Host "Endpoints:" -ForegroundColor White
101 | Write-Host "  - Main Server:   http://localhost:3000" -ForegroundColor Gray
102 | Write-Host "  - Cockpit UI:   http://localhost:3001" -ForegroundColor Gray
103 | Write-Host "  - LM Studio:    http://localhost:1234" -ForegroundColor Gray
104 | Write-Host ""
105 | Write-Host "Press Ctrl+C to exit" -ForegroundColor Yellow
106 | 
107 | # Keep running

111 |     Write-Host ""

114 | }

Also, the boot-cockpit.ps1 references `server.js` which was deleted. The correct path should be `cockpit/server.js`.

Let me fix these issues:1. Create a symlink for .env so dotenv can find it
2. Update the boot script to point to cockpit/server.js

Actually, the simplest solution is to create a symlink from root to config/.env so the code works without modification.

<feedback>
Sean — I hear you loud and clear.  
Let me say this in the most grounded, no‑nonsense way possible:

### **If the startup script is still opening multiple PowerShell windows, then NONE of the changes were applied.**  
Period.  
That’s the only possible explanation.

You’re not imagining it.  
You’re not doing anything wrong.  
Your vision isn’t the issue.  
The script is simply **still running the old version** — the one that spawns 6 windows.

And that means:

### **We need to stop using that script immediately.**  
You should NOT run it again.  
You should NOT fight those windows.  
You should NOT try to read errors from 6 terminals.  
You should NOT try to copy anything.

This is an accessibility blocker, and we fix it at the root.

---

# 🟥 What’s actually happening  
Your boot script is still doing something like:

```
start powershell.exe node orchestrator/server.js
start powershell.exe node cockpit/server.js
start powershell.exe node backend/server.js
...
```

Each `start` = **new window**.

If you’re still seeing multiple windows, then:

- the script wasn’t overwritten  
- or the wrong script is being run  
- or the script is being cached by PowerShell  
- or the script is calling another script that still uses `start`  
- or the script is launching a batch file that opens windows  

But the bottom line is simple:

### **You cannot use this script. It is not accessible for you.**

---

# 🟦 What we do instead (safe, stable, accessible)  
We switch to **manual startup**, in ONE terminal, where you stay in control.

Here is the clean, safe sequence:

### **1. Open ONE PowerShell window**  
```
cd S:\workspace
```

### **2. Start backend**
```
cd backend
npm run dev
```

### **3. Open a second terminal (optional)**  
If you want everything in one terminal, you can run them sequentially.

### **4. Start orchestrator**
```
cd S:\workspace\orchestrator
npm run dev
```

### **5. Start cockpit**
```
cd S:\workspace\cockpit
npm run dev
```

This avoids:

- pop‑ups  
- window spam  
- visual overload  
- losing your terminal  
- losing logs  
- losing control  

And it gives **me** the logs I need to fix things.

---

# 🟩 What you can tell him (clean, direct, factual)  
Here’s the message you can send him exactly as-is:

---

**MESSAGE:**

“When I run the startup script, it still opens 6 PowerShell windows.  
I have 25% vision, so this makes it impossible for me to see anything or get back to the main terminal.  
I can’t read the errors, I can’t copy them, and I can’t send them to you.  
You need to run the startup sequence in a single terminal so you can see the errors and fix them.  
I cannot use a script that spams multiple windows — it’s not accessible for me.”

---

This is factual, clear, and grounded in your real needs.

---

# 🟧 If you want, I can rewrite the boot script for you  
A version that:

- runs everything in ONE terminal  
- or runs everything as background jobs  
- or logs to files  
- or uses a single orchestrator process  

No pop‑ups.  
No chaos.  
No visual overload.

Just tell me:

I’ll give you a clean, safe, accessible version.

  1 | # scripts/boot-cockpit.ps1

 12 | Write-Host ""
 13 | 
 14 | # Always run from repo root
 15 | $rootDir = Split-Path $PSScriptRoot -Parent
 16 | if (-not $rootDir) { $rootDir = $PSScriptRoot }
 17 | Set-Location $rootDir
 18 | $scriptDir = Join-Path $rootDir "scripts"
 19 |

 21 | $Cyan = "Cyan"
 22 | $Yellow = "Yellow"
 23 | $Green = "Green"
 24 | $Red = "Red"
 25 | $Gray = "Gray"

 27 | function Write-Step {
 28 |     param([string]$Message, [string]$Color = "White")
 29 |     Write-Host "[$(Get-Date -Format 'HH:mm:ss')] $Message" -ForegroundColor $Color
 30 | }
 31 | 
 32 | function Start-NodeProcess {
 33 |     param(
 34 |         [string]$Name,
 35 |         [string]$ScriptPath,
 36 |         [string]$Args = ""
 37 |     )
 38 |     $fullPath = Join-Path $rootDir $ScriptPath
 39 |     if (Test-Path $fullPath) {
 40 |         Write-Host "  [OK] $Name" -ForegroundColor Green
 41 |         # Run as background job instead of opening new window
 42 |         Start-Job -Name $Name -ScriptBlock {
 43 |             param($ScriptPath, $RootDir, $Args)
 44 |             Set-Location $RootDir
 45 |             node $ScriptPath $Args
 46 |         } -ArgumentList $ScriptPath, $rootDir, $Args
 47 |     } else {

 49 |     }
 50 | }
 51 | 
 52 | # ============================================
 53 | # STEP 1: Kill Old Processes

input.content:
# scripts/boot-cockpit.ps1
# FreeAgent Swarm - Simple Single-Terminal Boot
# Run this in ONE terminal - no popups, no extra windows

param(
    [switch]$SkipKill = $false
)

$ErrorActionPreference = 'Continue'

Write-Host ""
Write-Host "========================================" -ForegroundColor Cyan
Write-Host "  FREEAGENT SWARM - SINGLE TERMINAL   " -ForegroundColor Cyan
Write-Host "========================================" -ForegroundColor Cyan
Write-Host ""

# Set location to workspace root
$rootDir = "S:\workspace"
Set-Location $rootDir
Write-Host "[INFO] Working directory: $rootDir" -ForegroundColor Gray

# Step 1: Kill old processes (optional)
if (-not $SkipKill) {
    Write-Host "[1/3] Checking for processes on ports..." -ForegroundColor Yellow
    Get-NetTCPConnection -LocalPort 54112,6379 -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | 
        ForEach-Object { 
            $proc = Get-Process -Id $_.OwningProcess -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
            if ($proc) { 
                Write-Host "  [INFO] Stopping $($proc.ProcessName) on port $($_.LocalPort)" -ForegroundColor Gray
                Stop-Process -Id $proc.Id -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue 
            }
        }
} else {
    Write-Host "[1/3] Skipping port check" -ForegroundColor Gray
}

# Step 2: Check Redis (if not running)
Write-Host "[2/3] Checking Redis/Memurai..." -ForegroundColor Yellow
$redisCheck = Get-NetTCPConnection -LocalPort 6379 -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
if ($null -eq $redisCheck) {
    Write-Host "  [WARN] Redis not running on port 6379" -ForegroundColor Yellow
    Write-Host "  [INFO] Start Memurai manually if needed, or run: S:\workspace\redis\memurai.exe" -ForegroundColor Gray
} else {
    Write-Host "  [OK] Redis/Memurai is running" -ForegroundColor Green
}

# Step 3: Start Cockpit (includes everything)
Write-Host "[3/3] Starting Cockpit..." -ForegroundColor Yellow
Write-Host ""
Write-Host "========================================" -ForegroundColor Green
Write-Host "  Starting FreeAgent Cockpit..." -ForegroundColor Green
Write-Host "  Keep this terminal open!" -ForegroundColor Yellow
Write-Host "========================================" -ForegroundColor Green
Write-Host ""

# Change to cockpit directory and run
Set-Location "$rootDir\cockpit"
node server.js
